payload.text: Hãy thiết kế giao diện Phần mềm quản lý sản phẩm & đơn hàng, dùng để kiểm soát bán hàng online trên facebook, woocormerce & Alibaba, Amazon, etsy. Kết nối các sàn thương mại điện tử bằng API (có tính năng cho điền API của sàn tự động). Có tính năng hiển thị danh sách sản phẩm ở các sàn, hình ảnh, giá tiền, giá sale,v.v.... Có tính năng đồng bộ đơn hàng, quản lý kho, sô lượng sản phẩm. Có tính năng AI trí tuệ nhân tạo để kiểm soát đơn hàng, tồn kho. Giao diện đơn giản, dễ sử dụng. tông màu sáng, có thanh menu bên trái và hướng dẫn sử dụng đơn giản. Có tính năng thanh toán online để nâng cấp thành viên, tính năng đăng nhập, đăng ký, quên mật khẩu.

**Developing the Application Idea**

I'm now zeroing in on the core functionality: a React-based application to juggle products and orders from various e-commerce platforms. The initial step of platform integration is complete, and I'm moving toward handling product information, and how that fits into an overall schema, and how orders will be presented. I'm focusing on the user interface next.


**Outlining Core Functionality**

I've been further refining the app's components, specifically focusing on product listing capabilities, the user interface design, and how the different product views will function. Integrating user authentication and a subscription upgrade feature is next. I'm prioritizing the dashboard overview to present the most relevant information to users, and planning out detailed page/module breakdowns.


**Planning Detailed Features**

I'm now zeroing in on the detailed functionalities. Order management and stock levels are top priority, along with AI insights via a Gemini chat interface. I'm also planning the API key input settings for platform integration, along with different upgrade plans. Finally, I'm working through the application's core structure, focusing on the layout and the different components to ensure a smooth user experience.


**Prioritizing AI Features**

I'm now focusing on AI integration. I've chosen `gemini-2.5-flash` for fast analysis, like stock predictions and anomaly detection, with `gemini-3-pro-preview` for more complex reasoning. I have set up the `mockData.ts` file, and am starting to work on the `geminiService.ts` now, using the new Google API. The idea is for the user to ask questions like "Which products are low on stock?", and the AI reads the mock data to answer.


**Constructing the Architecture**

I'm presently building the components structure in React, focusing on essential UI elements. I'm leveraging Tailwind CSS for a clean look, adopting a light blue/indigo color scheme. I've mapped out the `Sidebar`, `Header`, `Auth`, `Dashboard`, `Products`, `Orders`, `Integrations`, and `AIModule` components, and will move next to logic within each. Auth will simulate user login/registration. For the routing, I'm going to leverage a simple `activeTab` state in `App.tsx` instead of `react-router-dom`.


**Enhancing AI Integration**

I'm now integrating the AI advisor. I'll serialize the current dashboard data as part of the prompts to give Gemini contextual awareness for insights. I'm building a flex-row layout with a sidebar (64w) and main content (flex-1). The theme will be light with slate accents. I'm building out platform icons for Facebook, WooCommerce, Alibaba, Amazon, and Etsy and adding a sync button with a simulation spinner. I'll include a dedicated "AI Advisor" tab to use the inventory JSON data and provide answers to user queries.


**Listing out Project Structure**

I'm focusing now on the project's foundational structure. I've compiled a precise file list: `metadata.json`, `index.html`, `index.tsx`, `types.ts`, `services/mockData.ts`, `services/geminiService.ts`, plus essential components: `Sidebar`, `Dashboard` (with charts), `ProductList`, `OrderList`, `Integrations`, and `AIAdvisor`. I'm using `mockData.ts` to populate the UI effectively. The central logic will be in `geminiService.ts`, and I've reorganized to put logical sub-components in separate files.


**Completing Code Generation**

I'm now putting the final touches on code generation, focusing on component structure and detailed design. I've re-integrated the `recharts` for the dashboard, and am making the AI prompts more precise to minimize data hallucinations. I'm also ensuring the Membership section is visually appealing, and checking for TS compliance, API key usage, and import path correctness, as well as checking against all project constraints. I am now proceeding with detailed design, focusing on the visual and logical aspects, and making sure that all edge cases are addressed.


**Formulating the Gemini Prompt**

I'm now zeroing in on prompt construction for the AI. I'll pass in data from the current inventory, and the user's question, for contextual answers. It will use a format like: `Context: ${JSON.stringify(inventorySummary)}. User Question: ${userPrompt}`. I'm focusing on making the interaction more dynamic and contextually aware.

payload.text: Thêm chức năng thông báo đẩy (push notification) khi có tin nhắn mới trong ChatSystem. Thông báo nên hiển thị tên người gửi và một phần nội dung tin nhắn.

**Initiating Feature Design**

I'm currently fleshing out the core requirements for integrating push notifications within the `ChatSystem`. My focus is on defining the triggers and content. Specifically, I'm analyzing how to simulate new message arrivals to test notification behavior and how to dynamically construct the most useful, concise notification content for users.


**Evaluating Notification Delivery**

I've been drilling down on the technical implementation of the notification system. Since this is a front-end demo, I'm leaning toward simulating message arrivals using `setInterval` within `ChatSystem`. For the UI, native browser notifications are out due to potential blocking. Instead, I'm favoring an in-app "Toaster" notification, aligning with the app's React style and avoiding the drawbacks of native notifications within an iframe.


**Revising Notification Scope**

My current thinking is centered around how to make these notifications truly global, given the user's focus on `ChatSystem`. I've shifted from simulating notifications strictly within `ChatSystem.tsx`. Instead, I'm leaning toward a `NotificationSystem` component in `App.tsx` that listens for simulated events, ensuring notifications appear regardless of the user's active tab. This approach aligns better with the concept of push notifications. I'm exploring the best way to handle the simulation, whether directly in `App` or a global simulator, but the key is global reach, while making this behavior available to `ChatSystem`.


**Defining Core Components**

My current approach prioritizes a non-intrusive yet functional notification system. I'm focusing on a Toast component within `components/NotificationToast.tsx`, managing notification state globally within `App.tsx`. The core mechanic involves using `setInterval` in `App.tsx` to simulate incoming messages from random users in `MOCK_CONVERSATIONS`, triggering the Toast. While originally meant for `ChatSystem`, I will focus on making `App.tsx` the main handler for demo purposes to provide a more global view.


**Centralizing Notification Logic**

I'm now implementing the `NotificationSystem` to sit within `App.tsx`, providing global coverage. I'm building a `<NotificationToast>` component to handle the UI. Simulating message arrivals will be handled in `App.tsx` using `setInterval`, selecting random messages from a mock dataset. When clicked, this notification will switch the user to the "messages" tab. I'm also planning to incorporate optional native browser notifications if the user grants permission to add another layer.


**Incorporating Permissions Check**

I'm now incorporating permission logic into the `App.tsx` file for a more polished experience. The plan is to only trigger the notifications after checking for notification permissions to avoid user confusion. I will be adding a default message to the toast to improve usability.


**Implementing the Toast UI**

I'm focusing on refining the `NotificationToast` component. The design emphasizes clear sender identification and concise message previews using `slate-50` and `indigo-600` color scheme. I'm carefully positioning the toast to avoid obstructing the `FloatingAssistant` or the sidebar by defaulting to top-right. I'm also preparing a selection of realistic mock messages for the simulation. Finally, I will integrate the logic for optional browser notifications, ensuring graceful handling of denied permissions.


**Integrating Message Updates**

I've decided that auto-updating the chat list within `ChatSystem` isn't essential for this demo; the focus remains on global notifications. I'm now refining the simulated message logic in `App.tsx` by picking a random conversation from `MOCK_CONVERSATIONS`, using `incomingMessages`, and updating the last message in the Toast. I'm building this feature to complete my core feature requirements.

Alternatively, specifying the generic type for `reduce` would solve the issue too. I'm focusing on modifying the `newTotalStock` calculation to resolve the type mismatch and ensure a reliable sum.


**Resolving the Typings**

I've determined that the `unknown` issue originates from TypeScript's inference of `Object.values` on `newStocks`, even though I know it should be returning `number[]`.
